evidence: ExploreOptions record cleanly bundles 4 numeric tuning parameters, preventing parameter-list explosion at the handler→explorer seam
RelationUtils.resolveRelationAlias is the single translation point for alias → grammar-config-ID mapping; both ExplorationHandlers and SketchHandlers use it without reimplementing
buildExploreResponseBody() is shared between handleSemanticFieldExplore and handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti, so the JSON serialization seam is defined once

ExplorationResult used as the output contract for both exploreByPattern and exploreMultiSeed, but with incompatible semantics: in single-seed mode discoveredNouns contains nouns discovered in the field; in multi-seed mode discoveredNouns contains the seeds themselves
ExploreOptions.nounsPerSeed is unused in the multi-seed call path (confirmed by in-code comment 'nouns_per intentionally not supported in multi-seed mode')

handleFullSketchForType uses a boolean isDep flag to fork both query dispatch and response structure — the DEP path adds a 'type':'dependency' key and uses buildRelationResponse(), the SURFACE path builds an anonymous map inline

CORS origin 'http://localhost:3000' is hardcoded in 4 separate HttpApiUtils methods (sendJsonResponse, sendError, sendOptionsResponse, sendBinaryResponse) — the origin value is a cross-cutting boundary policy repeated rather than centralized

strengths: ExploreOptions record as a parameter-bundle eliminates a 5-arg method signature at the handler→explorer boundary
RelationUtils.resolveRelationAlias provides a single, explicit translation seam between HTTP alias strings and grammar-config IDs
buildExploreResponseBody() and buildBaseExploreResponse() factored as shared formatters, preventing response-shape duplication across single/multi-seed handlers
HttpApiUtils provides a minimal, consistent boundary layer: parseQueryParams, requireParam, sendJsonResponse cover the shared path without overfitting to any single handler
mid_level_elegance.issue_character: The dominant pattern is semantic overloading: ExplorationResult is reused for two structurally-different modes (single-seed discovery vs. multi-seed comparison), silently changing what discoveredNouns means and making the shared buildExploreResponseBody seam work by coincidence rather than contract.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: The handler→service→query layering is largely clean and the seam utilities are well-extracted. However, ExplorationResult's dual-mode overload is a genuine seam design flaw — callers cannot tell from the type what the discoveredNouns represent, and ExploreOptions carries a dead field in one mode. The isDep flag splitting response structure inside one method adds a second seam impurity. These are concentrated but impactful enough to place the codebase below a 70 on this dimension.

issues.summary: ExplorationResult overloaded for two incompatible semantics: discoveredNouns means discovered field members in single-seed mode but means input seeds in multi-seed mode
evidence: exploreByPattern adds DiscoveredNoun entries for nouns found in the semantic field of the seed
exploreMultiSeed populates discoveredNounsList with the seeds themselves: 'discoveredNounsList.add(new DiscoveredNoun(seed, sharedCollocs, ...))' (SemanticFieldExplorer.java:394)
buildExploreResponseBody() labels the section 'discovered_nouns' in the JSON response — in multi-seed mode this section contains the input seeds, not discovered nouns
ExplorationResult.getSeed() returns String.join(',', seeds) in multi-seed mode — a comma-joined string rather than a noun lemma
issues.suggestion: Introduce a separate MultiSeedExplorationResult (or a sealed variant) that names the 'seeds' field explicitly rather than reusing ExplorationResult. Alternatively, add a mode discriminant to ExplorationResult and rename the field to 'participants' in multi-seed mode. Update buildExploreResponseBody to branch on the mode when labeling JSON keys.

issues.summary: ExploreOptions.nounsPerSeed is silently ignored in the multi-seed call path, creating a dead parameter in the boundary contract
evidence: ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti resolves nounsPerSeed from exploreParams but never passes it to exploreMultiSeed (line 115+: 'nouns_per intentionally not supported in multi-seed mode' comment)
ExploreOptions record includes nounsPerSeed as a first-class field, suggesting it is part of the contract for all exploration calls
A caller constructing ExploreOptions for multi-seed use would reasonably expect the field to have effect
issues.suggestion: Either create a separate ExploreOptions variant without nounsPerSeed for multi-seed use, or document the field as SURFACE/single-seed-only in the record and add an assertion in exploreMultiSeed that the field is not relied upon. The current silent discard violates least-surprise at the parameter boundary.

issues.summary: Orphaned Javadoc for the 5-arg exploreByPattern overload sits before the 3-arg method's Javadoc, leaving the 5-arg method effectively undocumented
evidence: Lines 95-107: Javadoc with @param bcqlPattern, @param simplePattern, @param relationName — none of which are parameters of the immediately following 3-arg method
Lines 108-110: Second Javadoc 'Convenience overload...' is attached to the 3-arg method at line 109
The 5-arg method starting at line 121 has no Javadoc: in Java the last Javadoc before a declaration is attached, so the 5-arg method inherits nothing
issues.suggestion: Move the @param bcqlPattern/@param simplePattern Javadoc block to immediately precede the 5-arg method signature (line 121), and place the 3-arg convenience overload's Javadoc immediately before the 3-arg method.

issues.summary: isDep boolean flag in handleFullSketchForType controls both query dispatch and divergent response shape, conflating two separate seam concerns in one method
evidence: handleFullSketchForType checks 'isDep' twice: once to skip relations without deprel, and again to choose between buildRelationResponse() and an inline anonymous map

The SURFACE path builds relData inline with 'name', 'cql', 'collocate_pos_group', 'collocations' keys; the DEP path calls buildRelationResponse() which uses 'id', 'name', 'description', 'deprel', 'total_matches', 'collocations'

Response keys differ between the two paths: SURFACE includes 'cql' and 'collocate_pos_group', DEP includes 'id', 'description', 'deprel', 'total_matches' — the same method produces structurally different outputs depending on a flag
issues.suggestion: Split handleFullSketchForType into two private methods handleFullSurfaceSketch and handleFullDepSketch. Each method produces a coherent, single-responsibility response structure. The flag-based fork disappears and each branch becomes independently navigable.

issues.summary: CORS origin 'http://localhost:3000' hardcoded in 4 separate HttpApiUtils methods rather than defined once at the boundary
evidence: sendJsonResponse: exchange.getResponseHeaders().set('Access-Control-Allow-Origin', 'http://localhost:3000')
sendError: same literal repeated
sendOptionsResponse: same literal repeated
sendBinaryResponse: same literal repeated
Each occurrence has a comment '// Dev-only: restrict to specific origins in production' indicating this is known boundary policy, not local behavior
issues.suggestion: Extract the CORS origin to a single private constant (e.g., CORS_ORIGIN = 'http://localhost:3000') in HttpApiUtils, or better, pass it as a constructor-time configuration to the server. This makes the boundary policy visible and changeable in one place.

root_causes: ExplorationResult was designed for single-seed exploration and later reused for multi-seed without introducing a new type, spreading semantic ambiguity to every consumer of the model
HTTP boundary utilities evolved one method at a time rather than from a single CORS policy decision, causing the origin to be copy-pasted rather than referenced
likely_symptoms: exploration_result_dual_semantics is root; explore_options_dead_field is a symptom of the same design choice
